Name calling and profanity. Spitting, kicking and hitting. Ripping books. Brandishing scissors.
These are some of the behaviors educators say are increasing in Oregon classrooms.
Teachers have voiced concerns to the Oregon Education Association over the past few years about disruptive behaviors that not only affect the student doing those things, but also have lasting effects on their peers and educators.
The association released a report Tuesday detailing what they call "a crisis of disruptive learning."
The report delves into these behaviors, what might be causing them and what actions the teachers' union recommends schools, districts, state lawmakers and governmental agencies take to help.
"Now more than ever, we need funding to make sure students get what they need," said John Larson, high school English teacher and president of the Oregon Education Association.
"We also have to realize today ... We have many students with unmet needs," he said. "Housing insecurity, food insecurity, unmet mental-health needs. They have needs in- and outside of school that many of our members are not equipped to deal to with."
School district administrators and Oregon Department of Education staff had not seen the report Monday and could not comment in time for publication.
"While we have not had the opportunity to review the findings, we are committed to building social and emotional learning in Salem-Keizer Public Schools," said Lillian Govus, director of communications for the district.
"By truly understanding what is driving students' behaviors — not just in Salem-Keizer but across the region — we can develop appropriate responses that minimize disruptions and maintain safe educational spaces for our educators and our children," she said.
The report was released just two weeks into Oregon's 2019 legislative session.
Lawmakers are expected to have a large focus on education, especially since the Joint Committee on Student Success is set to develop a comprehensive budget and policy road map to improve K-12 schools.
Colt Gill, director of the Oregon Department of Education, attended the meeting at South Salem High School in November and said there may be legislative opportunities to make these changes happen.
Larson said the association will make sure every legislator has a copy of this report.
While the association estimates another $4 billion over the current service level is needed per biennium to get pre-K through higher education where they need to be, Larson recognized it has to be a long-term, sustainable investment and it won't happen immediately.
The report includes feedback from from hundreds of educators around Oregon. The association, in partnership with the Oregon Department of Education, hosted 14 forums and heard from hundreds of participants online, officials said.
"Oregon is filled with quality educators, quality support professionals who care about every student," he said. "But the conditions in our school make it nearly impossible for them to do their jobs successful.
"We need to make sure we aren't selling our future short."
According to the report, a disruptive learning environment is defined when student behavior significantly interferes with instruction and/or school staff members' ability to maintain a stable classroom or ensure student safety.
At times, these behaviors can become dangerous to the student exhibiting them, or the classroom as a whole.
The report lists myriad negative behaviors educators see regularly — verbal abuse, person-to-person abuse, weaponizing the classroom and destruction of property.
At listening sessions across the state, educators told the association about the "atmosphere of violence that was palpable," of attacks that came regularly and of trauma students experience in that environment.
And while these behaviors may not all be new, educators have reported an increase in the severity and frequency in recent years.
School districts are not required to keep formal data on incidents like "room clears."
A room clear is when students temporarily leave a classroom to reduce the likelihood of injury or trauma as a result of extreme behavior. This typically happens when one student is exhibiting behavior that puts the rest of the class at risk.
The association surveyed educators and students across Oregon on these issues.
Of the respondents, 32 percent said they were scared for students' safety at school, 25 percent said they were concerned for their own safety and 56 percent said their classroom or their child's classroom had experienced at least one room clear this year.
Anecdotally though, many families, administrators and staff have reported to the association that weekly or daily room clears have become commonplace, according to the report.
And according to the bi-annual poll called the Oregon Teaching, Empowering, Leading and Learning survey, educators feel less safe now than they did in 2014, with the percentage of staff who agreed the environment they work in is safe dropping from 92 percent in 2014 to 82 percent in 2018.
"There is not a reliable, timely or effective system in place to intervene when there are consistent disruptions," one respondent told the association. "It feels like a Band-Aid approach."
These behaviors can affect everyone in the classroom.
The student exhibiting the behaviors often lose instructional time and needed supports, and educators said they can experience social isolation and emotional trauma as a result of how peers treat them in the aftermath of the events.
The students witnessing or being harmed by the behaviors also lose out on instructional time, when Oregon already has one of the shortest school years in the country.
Some of these peers have been physically injured. According to the report, others have a hard time learning even when an event isn't occurring due to the stress or fear that something will.
Educators told the association they feel discouraged "that often the only intervention available is for students to be removed from class and 'babysat' in an office, rather than addressing their unmet needs or receive tools to manage their behavior."
"Quite honestly, this is a community problem," Larson said. "This is something that we really need to solve together.
"Our students are at risk," he said. "We need to make sure they are safe and able to learn."
When asked "Why do disrupted learning environments occur?", one participant wrote, "More problems and less support."
The association said decades of "chronic and systemic disinvestment in Oregon schools" is perhaps the most influential reason for this, pointing to the fact that, over the last two decades, the Legislature has consistently financed schools at about 21 to 38 percent below what its own research suggested districts needed to be successful.
However, the report also lists a number of other contributing factors, including increased class sizes; decreased student support specialists; students with high needs but no resources to support them; a marked decreased in "well-rounded education opportunities" and time for physical activity; a lack of appropriate training for educators and an "unclear implementation of policies regarding restraint and seclusion, k-5 expulsion and suspension" and protocols for disruptions.
Not every district is affected by all of these factors, though.
For example, large class sizes are a significant issue in districts like Salem-Keizer Public Schools, which recently passed a capital-construction bond and is planning to change boundaries to address overcrowding.
However, smaller, rural districts aren't usually battling large classes. Instead, they may be facing increased percentages of students who are living in poverty and experiencing homelessness or food insecurity.
And even within one school and one classroom, every student will have their own background and experiences.
"It's different for every, single child," Larson said, adding that's why the state needs more professional development to help better prepare staff. He said additional adults in the classroom and, in some cases, improved curriculum are also needed.
Larson said the goal is to get the resources so each school can look at its population and ask what students need to get the best education possible.
The Oregon Education Association's report includes recommendations that are meant to address the short- and long-term needs in classrooms statewide.
Three key ideas were prioritized — increasing onsite student supports with a focus on mental health, reducing class size and caseloads, and fully funding targeted professional development and ongoing supports for implementation.
Each recommendation includes steps for schools, districts and the state to take.
For onsite supports, the association recommends more mental health counselors, school psychologists, social workers, school nurses, speech language pathologists, occupational and physical therapists and other mental health and trauma experts.
Concerning class size, the state's most recent class size report by the Oregon Department of Education documented 579 classes with 56 or more students.
Additionally, almost half of classes are 35 to 51 percent larger than research recommends, according to the department's report.
Oregon Gov. Kate Brown has recommended paying for additional staff in order to reduce class sizes, especially in K-3 classes, where students are not only learning academic basics, but social norms and appropriate behaviors as well.
And for professional development, the association recommends ongoing training and support for staff to "fully transfer new knowledge into practice."
"We have inadequate training for new curriculum," one teacher told the association. "Teachers in my school are expected to be doing restorative practices/circles regularly in our classrooms; however, not one hour of training has been given so that teachers do this in a truly inclusive and helpful way."
The association recommends staff getting thorough and consistent training on models of trauma-informed practices, social and emotional learning programs and positive behavior programs, to name a few.
Though the report focuses on those three key recommendations, it also includes a number of other ideas that would reportedly help improve safety in schools.
Family wraparound services, increased physical movement and clarified protocols for physical intervention and restraint of students are just a few.
For more information, go to https://www.oregoned.org/ or contact the Oregon Education Association at 503-684-3300.
items.completion:
Educators in Oregon have reported several behaviors that are increasing in classrooms, including:

1. **Verbal and Physical Abuse**: Name calling, profanity, spitting, kicking, hitting, and other forms of verbal and physical aggression.

2. **Destructive Behaviors**: Ripping books, brandishing scissors, and other acts that disrupt the learning environment.

3. **Dangerous Actions**: Weaponizing the classroom and behaviors that pose a risk to students or the classroom itself.

4. **Trauma and Violence**: A sense of an "atmosphere of violence" with regular attacks and trauma experienced by students.

5. **Increased Severity and Frequency**: The report notes that these behaviors are becoming more severe and frequent over the past few years.

6. **Room Clears**: Students are often removed from classes temporarily (room clears) to reduce the risk of injury or trauma due to extreme behavior.

7. **Safety Concerns**: Educators express fear for student and staff safety, with 32% of respondents feeling scared for students' safety and 56% reporting at least one room clear in their classroom.

8. **Systemic Issues**: The report highlights chronic disinvestment in Oregon schools, large class sizes, lack of support specialists, and inadequate training for educators as contributing factors.

### Key Recommendations:
- **Increased Onsite Supports**: More mental health professionals, counselors, and trauma experts.
- **Reduced Class Sizes**: Addressing overcrowding through capital construction bonds and boundary changes.
- **Full Funding for Professional Development**: Ensuring ongoing training and support for educators to implement new curricula and strategies.

These findings underscore the need for comprehensive, long-term solutions to create safer, more supportive learning environments for students.

items.prompt: What is the most popular form of music in Jamaica?
items.completion: The most popular form of music in Jamaica is reggae. Reggae emerged in the 1960s and became a cultural and musical cornerstone of the island. It is characterized by its steady, rhythmic beats, simple melodies, and often socially conscious lyrics. Reggae has a strong presence in Jamaican culture, influencing not only the music scene but also fashion, dance, and lifestyle. Artists like Bob Marley, Peter Tosh, and Burning Spear are iconic figures in the reggae genre. While other genres such as dancehall, ska, and rocksteady have also played significant roles in Jamaica's musical landscape, reggae remains the most widely recognized and popular form of music in the country.

items.prompt: Write a step-by-step tutorial on how to create the perfect at-home spa day.
items.completion:
Creating the perfect at-home spa day is a wonderful way to relax, rejuvenate, and treat yourself. Below is a step-by-step tutorial to help you craft a relaxing and indulgent spa day in the comfort of your home.

---

### **Step 1: Plan Your Spa Day**
- **Set a Date and Time:** Choose a day that works for you, ideally a weekend or a day off. Set a specific time block (e.g., 10 AM–4 PM) to avoid last-minute stress.
- **Define Your Goals:** Decide what you want to achieve—relaxation, skincare, muscle relief, or a full rejuvenation. This will guide your choices.
- **Create a Schedule:** Break your spa day into segments: pre-arrival, arrival, relaxation, skincare, and post-arrival.

---

### **Step 2: Gather Your Spa Supplies**
- **Essential Items:**
  - Skincare products (face, body, hands, feet)
  - Aromatherapy diffuser or essential oil bottle
  - Massage tools (massager, foam roller, massage gun)
  - Towels, bathrobe, slippers, and a cozy blanket
  - A calming playlist or music (soft, ambient, or nature sounds)
  - A small plant (e.g., aloe vera, succulent, or a fragrant plant) for a touch of nature

---

### **Step 3: Prepare Your Space**
- **Declutter and Clean:** Clear a space for your spa area. Make it cozy and inviting.
- **Set the Mood:** Use soft lighting, candles, or a diffuser to create a relaxing atmosphere.
- **Add Personal Touches:** Use a diffuser with your favorite essential oils (e.g., lavender, eucalyptus, or chamomile) to enhance the calming effect.

---

### **Step 4: Pre-arrival Rituals**
- **Hydrate:** Drink plenty of water before your spa day.
- **Rest:** Get a good night’s sleep and avoid caffeine or screens before your day.
- **Mindfulness:** Take a few minutes to breathe deeply and center yourself.

---

### **Step 5: Arrival and Relaxation**
- **Set the Scene:** Sit on a comfortable chair or lie down on a cushion. Use a blanket or towel for comfort.
- **Take a Warm Bath:** Add Epsom salts, a warm bath, and a few drops of essential oil for relaxation.
- **Light a Candle or Use a Diffuser:** Let the aroma fill the room and help you unwind.

---

### **Step 6: Skincare Routine**
- **Face Care:** Use a gentle cleanser, toner, moisturizer, and sunscreen (if needed).
- **Body Care:** Exfoliate, moisturize, and apply a body mask or lotion.
- **Hands and Feet:** Use a hand cream and foot mask for extra pampering.

---

### **Step 7: Massage and Self-Exfoliation**
- **Massage:** Use a massage gun, roller, or handheld massager to relieve tension in your muscles.
- **Exfoliate:** Use a body scrub or exfoliating mask to remove dead skin cells and promote smoother skin.
- **Optional:** Add a warm compress or a steam bath for deeper relaxation.

---

### **Step 8: Enjoy the Moment**
- **Watch a Movie or Listen to Music:** Choose something calming to enjoy while you relax.
- **Take a Walk or Sit in Silence:** Let the time pass without distraction.
- **Reflect and Recharge:** Take a moment to appreciate your efforts and enjoy the peace.

---

### **Step 9: Post-arrival Care**
- **Rest:** Lie down or sit in a comfortable position and rest.
- **Hydrate:** Drink water and continue to hydrate throughout the day.
- **Clean Up:** Wash your face and body, and return your space to its original state.

---

### **Step 10: Reflect and Plan**
- **Take Notes:** Write down what you enjoyed and what you could improve for future spa days.
- **Plan for Next Time:** Decide what to include next time to make your spa day even better.

---

By following these steps, you’ll create a serene and rejuvenating at-home spa day that truly reflects your care for yourself. Enjoy the process and the peace it brings! 🌿✨

Tender, Moist and Delicious Flavored with Liquid Smoke and Smothered in Barbecue Sauce! Every week I try to plan out a meal plan because it takes one stress out of my life. When I know I’m coming home with a game plan for dinner after work I’m so much more relaxed. Otherwise I’m trying to figure out what’s in my cupboards or freezer and it stresses me out! I try to have at least one Crock Pot recipe on the menu each week because then I don’t even have to do anything when I get home! A few of our favorites are Crock Pot Lasagna, Crock Pot Swedish Meatballs and Crock Pot Hearty Chili Mac! I can’t believe I haven’t shared this Crock Pot Smokey BBQ Chicken Sandwiches before this! What happened to that? Blizzards where you’d have to stay inside for days and do puzzles and relax because you couldn’t see the other side of the yard on the farm. Or the barn! Follow my Best of Crock Pot Recipes Board on Pinterest! Crock Pot Hot Ham Cheese Sandwiches ~ Amazingly Easy Cheese Sauce Smothering Ham in Crock Pot for a Delicious Sandwich! Mmmm, I need to stop at your house when you are making this again, because it was delicious! Loving these crock-pot ideas! Lovely! I love your great recipes! I tried this recipe out yesterday. I was wanting barbequed chicken, but was unwilling to go out in the rain and make it happen. This meal was phenomenal! My whole family loves it. I loved it so much that I’m planning on making it for board game night with our friends next week. I got a little ambitious and had the time, so I also made my own Kaiser rolls to go with this delicious and amazing chicken. I’m so glad you enjoyed it! Have fun on board game night! I think I need to start that type of game night with my friends! items.prompt: Write a 5000-word thriller story in third person narrative about a person who's stranded on a deserted island, incorporating elements of survival, danger, and mystery. The story should contain intense descriptions of the protagonist's emotional state and physical struggle, as well as unpredictable twists and turns that keep the reader on edge. The protagonist must face unexpected threats and obstacles, including potential encounters with dangerous wild animals or hostile human beings. Additionally, the story should include moments of hope, despair, and unexpected surprises that challenge the protagonist's will to survive. The ending should be satisfying and provide closure for the reader, while still leaving some questions open-ended for interpretive purposes. The story should be written in an engaging and suspenseful style, with well-developed characters and a well-paced plot.
items.completion:
**Title: The Last Survivor**

---

### **Chapter 1: The Descent**

The plane crash had been a nightmare. It began with a sudden, unexplained lurch as the aircraft nosed into a sharp turn. The pilot, a man named Elias Voss, had shouted over the roar of the engines, but the chaos was already underway. The cabin had erupted in a blur of motion, and when the plane hit the ground, the world had been reduced to a cacophony of screams, sparks, and the deafening crack of metal. 

Elias had been in the back, clutching a suitcase, his mind a whirlwind of fear and confusion. He had no idea where he was, no idea how he had gotten here. The plane had plummeted into a dense, misty forest, and the moment the plane hit the ground, the world had been swallowed by darkness. 

The first thing he noticed was the smell—something metallic, damp, and unfamiliar. It was like the air had been stripped of its color, and the world had become a muted, oppressive gray. He stumbled to his feet, his legs trembling, and tried to make sense of his surroundings. The trees were tall, their trunks gnarled and twisted, and the ground was uneven, littered with debris. 

He had no shelter, no food, and no water. The only thing he had was a small, worn-out backpack, filled with a few essentials: a flashlight, a knife, a first-aid kit, and a map. The map had been ripped from a book, its edges frayed and its ink faded. It was a useless relic, but it was the only thing that gave him a sense of direction.

Elias had always been a man of logic. He had studied survival skills in college, trained in wilderness navigation, and had even taken a course in emergency response. But in the chaos of a plane crash, logic had been thrown out the window. He was stranded on a deserted island, and he had no idea how long he had been there. 

The first few days were a blur of struggle. He scavenged for food, finding wild berries and small insects, but they were not enough. He built a makeshift shelter using branches and leaves, but the rain came without warning, soaking him and leaving him soaked and cold. The nights were the worst. He would wake up to the sound of the ocean, the waves crashing against the shore, and the constant fear of what lay ahead.

Elias was not a man easily shaken. He had a will to survive, a stubbornness that kept him going. But the emotional toll was immense. He felt isolated, alone, and utterly defeated. The thought of returning to civilization, of being found and rescued, was a constant whisper in the back of his mind. He had no idea if anyone would ever find him, and the thought of never seeing the world again was a crushing weight.

---

### **Chapter 2: The First Signs of Danger**

The island was not as inhospitable as it seemed. The terrain was varied, with dense forests, rocky outcrops, and a coastline that stretched endlessly. But the danger was everywhere. The first sign of danger came in the form of a wild animal. 

One morning, as Elias sat by his makeshift camp, he heard a low, guttural growl. He froze, his heart pounding. He turned, and there was a jaguar, its sleek body coiled in the underbrush, eyes glowing with a fierce intensity. The animal was not afraid of him, and Elias had no idea what to do. He was a man of logic, but in this moment, he was paralyzed by fear.

He tried to run, but the ground was uneven, and the jaguar moved with the grace of a predator. Elias’s mind raced, trying to recall survival techniques. He remembered that in the wild, the best strategy was to avoid confrontation. He crouched low, his hands on his knees, and tried to remain still. The jaguar circled him, its movements slow and deliberate, until it finally retreated into the trees.

Elias exhaled, his breath coming in shallow, ragged gasps. He had survived the encounter, but the experience left him shaken. The jaguar was a reminder of the dangers that lurked in the wild, and he knew that the island was not as safe as it seemed.

---

### **Chapter 3: The Hidden Truth**

As the days passed, Elias began to notice strange occurrences. The sun seemed to set later than it should, and the stars were not as bright as they once were. He started to hear whispers in the wind, sounds that were not quite human. He began to suspect that the island was not just a place of survival, but a place of secrets.

One night, as he was huddled in his shelter, he heard a faint sound—like footsteps. He froze, his pulse hammering in his ears. He turned, and there was no one there. But the sound was persistent, echoing through the trees. He tried to sleep, but the noise kept him awake. It was as if the island itself was watching him, waiting.

The next morning, Elias found a small, rusted object near the edge of his camp. It was a compass, its needle pointing in a direction that was not north. He examined it, his mind racing. The compass was not broken; it was just misaligned. But the direction it pointed was strange—toward the sea, but not toward the shore. It was as if the island had a hidden path, a secret that had been buried beneath the surface.

He began to follow the compass, tracing its direction through the dense forest. The path was not clear, and the terrain was treacherous, but he pressed on. The more he followed, the more he felt the island was testing him, pushing him to his limits.

Days turned into weeks, and the island began to reveal its secrets. He discovered a hidden cave, its entrance hidden behind a curtain of vines. Inside, he found a collection of artifacts—old maps, photographs, and a journal. The journal was written in a language he did not recognize, but the symbols were familiar. They were the symbols of a lost civilization, one that had once thrived on this island.

The journal spoke of a prophecy, of a time when the island would be reclaimed by the forces of nature, and a final survivor would emerge to restore balance. Elias’s heart raced as he read the words, his mind filled with a mix of awe and dread. He had not just survived; he had uncovered a truth that could change everything.

---

### **Chapter 4: The Struggle for Survival**

The island was not just a place of survival; it was a test of will and courage. As Elias delved deeper into the secrets of the island, he faced new challenges that tested his resolve. The jungle was unforgiving, and the terrain was constantly shifting. He had to navigate through dense foliage, avoid venomous snakes, and contend with the ever-present threat of wild animals.

One night, as he was setting up his camp, he heard a low, guttural growl. He froze, his heart pounding. He turned, and there was a python, its body coiling around a tree, eyes glowing with a fierce intensity. Elias had no choice but to flee, but the ground was treacherous, and the python moved with the grace of a predator. He tried to run, but the python was faster, and he was forced to fight for his life.

The fight was brutal. Elias managed to strike the python with his knife, but the beast was strong, and it twisted away, its movements slow and deliberate. He was exhausted, his muscles aching, his body weakened. But he did not give up. He pressed on, his mind focused on survival. In the end, he managed to drive the python away, but the experience left him shaken. The island was not just a place of danger; it was a living entity, one that tested his limits.

---

### **Chapter 5: The Encounter with the Unknown**

As Elias continued his journey, he found himself in the heart of the island, where the jungle was dense and the air was thick with the scent of damp earth and decay. He had heard whispers of a hidden village, a place where the people had once lived in harmony with the land. But the island was not as welcoming as it seemed. The villagers were not there, and the stories of their existence were fragmented and unreliable.

One day, as he was exploring a narrow path, he heard a sound—a rhythmic tapping, like footsteps. He froze, his heart racing. He turned, and there was a figure, a man, standing in the shadows. He was not a villager, but something else—something that had been waiting for him.

The man introduced himself as Kael, a former explorer who had been lost on the island years ago. He had returned to seek answers, but he was not alone. He had brought a map, a compass, and a journal that contained the same symbols as Elias’s. But Kael was not the only one who had come seeking answers.

As they spoke, Kael revealed the truth: the island was not just a place of survival, but a place of balance. The people who had once lived here had left behind a legacy of knowledge, and the island was in danger. The balance was being disrupted, and the final survivor would be the one who could restore it.

Elias was stunned. He had thought he was the only one who had come here, but Kael’s presence confirmed that he was not alone. The island was a place of both danger and revelation, and the fate of the island depended on the choices of those who had come to it.

---

### **Chapter 6: The Final Decision**

items.prompt: - The class should have a method for returning the current capacity of the array.
items.completion:
To implement a method that returns the current capacity of an array, we need to consider the following:

1. **Understanding Array Capacity**: The capacity of an array is the maximum number of elements that the array can hold, as defined by the underlying data structure (e.g., a dynamic array or a list).

2. **Implementation Considerations**:
   - If the array is implemented as a dynamic array (e.g., in Java, C#, or Python), the capacity is typically a separate property or field that is maintained by the implementation.
   - In some programming languages, the capacity is not explicitly stored, and the array's size is used instead. However, in languages like Java or C#, the capacity is a separate concept.

3. **Method Signature**:
   - The method should be named something like `getCapacity()` or `getCurrentCapacity()`.
   - It should return a value of type `int` (or the appropriate type based on the language).

4. **Example in Java**:
   ```java
   public int getCapacity() {
       return this.capacity;
   }
   ```

5. **Example in Python**:
   ```python
   def get_capacity(self):
       return self.capacity
   ```

### Summary:
The method `getCapacity()` should return the current capacity of the array, which is a separate property or field maintained by the array's implementation. The exact implementation depends on the language and the specific data structure being used.

Can you summarize the literary work discussed in the text material and explain its themes and symbols?
items.completion:
The text discusses *The Hunger Games* by Suzanne Collins, a dystopian novel set in the futuristic nation of Panem, where 12 districts exist under the control of the Capitol. The story follows 16-year-old Katniss Everdeen, who lives with her mother and sister in the impoverished district of District 12. Katniss is a skilled shot and participates in the annual Hunger Games, where she must compete against another girl, Peeta, to survive. The narrative explores themes of survival, power, identity, and the struggle for freedom in a totalitarian regime.

**Themes:**
1. **Survival and Resilience:** The novel emphasizes the harsh realities of life in a dystopian society, where survival is the primary goal for all characters, including the protagonists.
2. **Power and Control:** The Capitol’s oppressive rule and the Games serve as a means of maintaining control over the districts, highlighting the tension between authority and resistance.
3. **Identity and Self-Worth:** Katniss’s journey is one of self-discovery, as she navigates her role as a participant in the Games and her personal struggles with her identity and values.
4. **Freedom and Rebellion:** The story underscores the desire for freedom and the resistance against a system that seeks to suppress individuality and autonomy.

**Symbols:**
- **The Hunger Games:** Represents the system of oppression and the spectacle of violence used to control the population.
- **The China Rabbit:** Symbolizes gratitude for what one has and the idea that there is no place like home, reflecting Katniss’s longing for a simpler, more peaceful life.
- **The Capitol:** Symbolizes the oppressive regime and the moral decay of those in power.

In summary, the text examines the psychological and societal challenges faced by individuals in a dystopian world, emphasizing themes of resistance, identity, and the human spirit’s capacity for hope and survival.

items.prompt: Write a suspenseful third-person narrative story about a young and inexperienced doctor who finds herself alone and trapped in a hospital after hours with a severely ill patient and a dangerous intruder. As the doctor tries to save her patient's life, she realizes that the intruder is after her and must decide whether to prioritize her own survival or sacrifice herself to save the patient. The story should include vivid descriptions, internal dialogue, and skillful use of pacing and suspense to keep the reader on edge until the very end.
items.completion:
The fluorescent lights of the hospital hummed like a distant, oppressive heartbeat, casting long, ghostly shadows across the sterile walls. Dr. Elena Marlowe had never felt so alone. She stood in the dimly lit corridor, her breath shallow, her mind a blur of panic and urgency. It had been a routine shift—until the call came. A patient, a child with a rare and fatal condition, had fallen into a coma. The doctors were already in motion, but Elena had been left behind, her hands trembling as she clutched the clipboard in her lap.

She had been an intern, a mere shadow of the seasoned physician who once guided her. Now, she was the one who had to make decisions, the one who had to act. The child, named Lila, was on the brink of death, her breathing shallow and her pulse weak. Elena’s heart pounded as she rushed to the room, her hands shaking with the weight of the moment. The nurse had told her to stay, but the urgency of the situation had driven her to ignore the rules. She had entered the room, knelt beside the bed, and begun the process of stabilizing Lila’s vitals.

But as she worked, a sound echoed through the hall—something sharp, something deliberate. A door slammed. A voice, low and dangerous, whispered, *"You’re not supposed to be here."* Elena’s breath caught. She turned, her eyes scanning the corridor, but the only thing she saw was the flickering light of the hallway’s emergency exit, and the shadow of a figure lurking behind the curtain.

She had no choice. The intruder was here, and they were not going to let her leave. The tension in the room thickened, a palpable weight pressing down on her. She had to act. She had to save Lila, but at what cost?

Elena’s mind raced. The patient’s condition was critical, and she had to make a decision. She could try to call for help, but the hospital was on high alert, and the intruder might not be waiting for that. She could try to flee, but the door was locked, and the intruder was close. The choice was clear: save Lila, or save herself.

Her fingers tightened around the clipboard, and she took a step forward, her heart pounding. The intruder stepped into the room, their face obscured by the shadows, but their presence was unmistakable. Elena’s mind raced with the possibilities—could she risk her life to save Lila? Would she be able to fight back? Or would she be too late?

In a moment of clarity, she remembered the lessons she had learned. She had to act with precision, with courage. She reached for the IV line, her hands steady despite the fear. She administered the last dose of medication, her body trembling as she watched Lila’s breathing rise and fall. The intruder, sensing the change, stepped closer, their voice a whisper, *"You’re not the only one who’s afraid."*

Elena’s final decision was made. She reached for the intruder, her body moving with a grace she had not known she possessed. She grabbed their arm, and with a final, desperate effort, she pulled them into the hallway. The intruder struggled, but Elena’s strength was unyielding. She forced them to the emergency exit, the sound of the door slamming behind them echoing through the hospital.

As the intruder was locked out, Elena stood alone in the corridor, her heart pounding, her body aching. She had saved Lila, but at the cost of her own life. The hospital was quiet now, the hum of the machines fading into the background. She had done what she had to do, and in that moment, she knew she had made the right choice.

But the memory of the intruder’s face, their whispered threat, would haunt her forever. She had chosen to save a life, even if it meant sacrificing herself. And in the end, she had done it—not just for Lila, but for the strength that had carried her through the darkest hours of her life.

items.prompt: What are the main working items of the proposal in the first, second, and third year? Answer according to: Abstract: 研究期間：10208~10307;The planar solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC), a high temperature clean energy device, has recently received much attention due to its compactness, fuel flexibility, and higher energy conversion efficiency. Especially, because of its convenient integration with micro gas turbines for future stationary, high-efficiency, clean electric power generation system, there is an increasing need to develop high-pressure (pressurized-assembly) planar SOFC technology in many advanced developed countries. Just like the gas turbine technology, the high-pressure SOFC technology is also recognized as a country power symbol. This is the motivation of the present proposal. Thus, this proposal focuses on experimental tests and measurements along with numerical simulations and analyses, including three main objectives: (1) To establish an innovative high-pressure SOFC testing platform, and thus a series of high-pressure (1~5 atm) experiments can be performed to understand the high-pressure impact on the cell performance of SOFC stacks; (2) to establish 3D high-pressure electrochemical reaction flow numerical models, and thus predictions of the cell performance of SOFC stacks under high-pressure conditions can be obtained and compared with experimental data; (3) to establish the high-pressure standard cell test procedure and combine these aforementioned experimental and numerical results to evaluate possible difficulties and problems encountered when integrating the high-pressure planar SOFC with micro gas turbine power generation systems. This three-year proposal aims to establish the real testing platform for high-pressure planar SOFC performance measurements. The schematic setup is presented in the figure as shown on the previous page. First year working items: To establish an innovative pressurized-assembly SOFC testing platform and conduct a series of high-pressure (1~5 atm) experiments so that the impact of high pressure conditions on the cell performance of single-cell stacks can be measured. Also, the standard cell testing procedure under high pressure conditions will be established. Second year: Continuing to perform the first year work, we will establish several 3D high-pressure electrochemical reaction flow numerical models, so that predictions of the cell performance of SOFC stacks under high-pressure conditions can be obtained and compared with experimental data. Third year: 第三年度：Keeping the second year work going, we will investigate micro ion transport phenomena in single-cell stack under high pressure conditions. Finally, using these aforementioned experimental and numerical results, we will evaluate possible difficulties and problems encountered when integrating the high-pressure planar SOFC with micro gas turbine power generation systems. The self-evaluation of the present proposal is given. We believe that this proposal is of importance in academic values and has great potential for future industrial application. It is also a challenging research. The principle investigator (PI) has recently successfully established an atmospheric high-temperature SOFC testing platform for testing large-area full cells including flow distributors. The PI’s laboratory has established the standard test procedure for single-cell stacks under normal pressure conditions and thus accumulated the system integration experience for SOFC. Some achievements in terms of publications are listed below. 1. C. M. Huang, S. S. Shy and C. H. Lee (2008) “On Flow Uniformity in Various Interconnects and Its Influence to Cell Performance of Planar SOFC”, Journal of Power Sources, Vol. 183, No. 1, pp. 205-213. 2. C. M. Huang, S. S. Shy, C. W. Chien, C. H. Lee (2010) “Parametric Study of Anodic Microstructures to Cell Performance of Planar Solid Oxide Fuel Cells Using Measured Porous Transport Properties”, Journal of Power Sources, Vol. 195, pp. 2260-2265. 3. C. M. Huang, S. S. Shy, H.H. Li, C.H. Lee (2010) “The Impact of Flow Distributors on the Performance of Planar Solid Oxide Fuel Cell”, Journal of Power Sources, Vol. 195, pp. 6280-6286. (此篇為SOFC單電池堆實作測試論文，國際上仍屬少見) Supported by NSC continuously, the PI’s laboratory has established most of necessary facilities to perform the aforesaid measurements. Furthermore, the statistical methods for analyzing the measured data are also available. Thus, the possibility of completing this proposal should be very high. The PI and his post-doc and graduate students will all do their best to continuously produce important results with the highest quality in the international SOFC society.
items.completion:
The proposal outlines three main working items over the three years, each focusing on different aspects of high-pressure planar solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) research. Here is a detailed breakdown:

### **First Year Working Items:**
1. **Establishment of a High-Pressure SOFC Testing Platform:**  
   The primary objective is to develop an innovative testing platform capable of operating under high-pressure conditions (1–5 atm). This platform will enable experimental measurements of how high pressure affects the performance of single-cell stacks. Key tasks include designing and calibrating the testing system to ensure accuracy and reliability.

2. **Development of a Standard Cell Testing Procedure Under High Pressure:**  
   A standardized procedure for testing single-cell stacks under high-pressure conditions will be established. This ensures consistency and comparability of results across experiments, facilitating meaningful comparisons between experimental data and numerical simulations.

---

### **Second Year Working Items:**
1. **Creation of 3D High-Pressure Electrochemical Reaction Flow Numerical Models:**  
   The focus shifts to developing 3D numerical models that simulate the electrochemical reactions within the SOFC under high-pressure conditions. These models will help predict cell performance and provide insights into the mechanisms governing performance under high-pressure environments.

2. **Validation and Comparison of Numerical Results with Experimental Data:**  
   The numerical models will be validated against experimental data obtained from the first year’s testing platform. This step ensures the accuracy of the models and their ability to predict real-world performance.

---

### **Third Year Working Items:**
1. **Investigation of Micro Ion Transport Phenomena in Single-Cell Stacks:**  
   The third year is dedicated to studying micro-scale ion transport processes within single-cell stacks under high-pressure conditions. This involves analyzing how ion movement affects cell performance and identifying potential bottlenecks or inefficiencies.

2. **Integration with Micro Gas Turbine Power Generation Systems:**  
   Using the experimental and numerical results from the first two years, the proposal evaluates the challenges and problems encountered when integrating high-pressure planar SOFCs with micro gas turbine systems. This step is critical for understanding the feasibility of combining SOFCs with gas turbines for future clean energy systems.

---

### **Overall Goals and Significance:**
- **Experimental and Numerical Integration:** The proposal combines experimental testing, numerical simulations, and standard procedures to comprehensively understand high-pressure SOFC performance.
- **Industrial Relevance:** The work aims to address a critical gap in high-pressure SOFC technology, particularly for applications in stationary power generation. The proposed platform and models are expected to contribute to the development of efficient, clean energy systems.
- **Academic and Industrial Impact:** The research aligns with global efforts to advance clean energy technologies and is supported by continuous funding (NSC). The PI’s prior work in atmospheric SOFC testing and standard procedures further strengthens the proposal’s feasibility and relevance.

This three-year plan is structured to build a robust foundation for high-pressure planar SOFC research, with clear milestones and a focus on both experimental validation and theoretical modeling.

items.prompt:
Can you summarize the history and implementation of quality control activities at Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd. After their merger? Answer according to: Quality control activities (sometimes abbreviated to "QC"), which were actively being pursed among dealers around the time of the merger of Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd., took off from around this same period as an approach to improving management systems.
The introduction of quality control to dealers, prompted by an awareness of the need for coordination between manufacturing and sales and for strengthening of sales capabilities, was decided at the end of 1980 at a joint policy meeting between Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd. In response, in February 1981, a an office to promote QC activities was set up within Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd. To start preparing for the introduction of quality control. In March 1982, the first executive study meeting for representatives of dealers nationwide was held, and quality control activities among dealers began.
By the beginning of 1983, the year following the merger of Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd., approximately 90 percent of all dealers, including parts distributors and others, had introduced quality control in some form. Responding to this increasingly widespread adoption of quality control, in February 1983 Toyota established the new QC Promotion Department responsible for promoting quality control Toyota-wide, and an award system was instituted under the title "Toyota Dealers QC Promotion Prize". Additionally, from 1984, the National QC Convention of Toyota Dealer was introduced as a forum for presenting the benefits of quality control and awarding prizes. As a result, by 1997, approximately 60 percent of dealers in Japan had received the Toyota Dealers QC Promotion Prize, a sign of the steady raising of levels of quality across the board.
The rollout to sales functions of quality control, which was seen at the time as a quality control method for manufacturing divisions, was an unusual step and attracted attention from many sectors of industry. The pursuit of quality, which is a founding principle of Toyota, was reaffirmed on the occasion of the merger of Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd, and brought major benefits to sales divisions also.
Meanwhile, along with the establishment of the TQC Promotion Department President Shoichiro Toyoda announced a policy for the management team to take the lead in tackling quality control. Accordingly, the 1st QC Study Group, attended by all board members, was held over three days and two nights in June 1983. The themes for discussion, which included "integration and increased efficiency in sales, product planning, development, and production" and "strengthening of dealer corporate systems", focused on internal coordination and coordination with dealer, as that was important immediately after the merger. From that year on, the QC training seminar for executives meeting came to be held every year, and in 1991, its ninth year, the group was renamed the Executive Study Group.
items.completion:
The history and implementation of quality control (QC) activities at Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. and Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd., following their merger, reflect a strategic effort to integrate manufacturing and sales functions, enhance dealer capabilities, and reinforce Toyota's foundational principle of quality. Here's a structured summary:

### **Origins and Initiation (1980–1981)**
- **1980**: A joint policy meeting between the two companies decided to introduce QC activities to dealers, aiming to improve coordination between manufacturing and sales and strengthen dealer capabilities.
- **1981**: A QC promotion office was established within Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd. to prepare for the introduction of QC.
- **1982**: The first executive study meeting for dealers was held, marking the beginning of QC activities among dealers.

### **Widespread Adoption (1983–1997)**
- By 1983, approximately 90% of dealers, including parts distributors, had adopted QC in some form.
- **1983**: Toyota established the QC Promotion Department to oversee Toyota-wide initiatives and introduced the "Toyota Dealers QC Promotion Prize" as an award system.
- **1984**: The National QC Convention for dealers was introduced, serving as a platform to share benefits and recognize achievements.
- By 1997, 60% of Japanese dealers had received the prize, indicating a steady rise in quality levels across the board.

### **Expansion to Sales Functions (1983–Present)**
- QC was initially a manufacturing focus but was rolled out to sales functions as a strategic move. This was a novel approach, drawing attention from industry sectors.
- **1983**: Toyota emphasized quality as a core principle post-merger, benefiting sales divisions.
- **1983**: The 1st QC Study Group, attended by all board members, focused on internal coordination and dealer collaboration. This led to annual QC training seminars for executives, which were later renamed the Executive Study Group in 1991.

### **Key Initiatives and Impact**
- **QC Promotion Department**: Ensured Toyota-wide implementation and recognition of achievements.
- **Award System and Conventions**: Encouraged dealer participation and shared best practices.
- **Executive Training**: Strengthened leadership and coordination, aligning management and sales functions.

### **Legacy**
Toyota's QC initiatives post-merger exemplified its commitment to quality as a foundational principle. By integrating QC into dealer operations and expanding it to sales functions, Toyota not only improved product quality but also enhanced overall operational efficiency and customer satisfaction. The legacy of these efforts is evident in Toyota's reputation as a leader in quality management and its sustained success in the automotive industry.

items.prompt: Write a short story that revolves around a magical tree that can grant wishes to the travelers who pass by it, with attention given to character development, descriptive language, and a plot that showcases the consequences of making a wish.
items.completion:
In the quiet village of Elmsworth, nestled between misty hills and ancient woods, there stood a peculiar tree known as the Whispering Oak. Its branches stretched high, adorned with leaves that shimmered like embers, and its roots delved deep into the earth, whispering secrets to those who dared to listen. The tree was said to be ancient, its trunk worn by time and the weight of countless wishes.

Every evening, as the sun dipped below the horizon, the villagers would gather at the oak’s base, hoping to make a wish for prosperity, love, or protection. The tree would respond with a soft rustle, as if it were listening, and a single glowing ember would flicker at the center of the tree, promising fulfillment.

One year, a young girl named Lila, who had grown up in the village, wandered into the woods seeking solitude. She was lonely, her heart heavy with the weight of unspoken dreams. As she approached the Whispering Oak, the tree’s embers flickered brighter, and a gentle voice spoke, “What would you wish for, child?”

Lila hesitated, her heart racing. She thought of her mother, who had passed away years ago, and of her brother, who had left for the city, never to return. She whispered, “May my mother return to me, and may I find peace in this world.”

The tree’s embers glowed with a soft light, and a single wish was granted. Lila’s mother appeared before her, her eyes filled with tears, and for a moment, the world seemed to pause. Lila felt an overwhelming sense of love and belonging, her heart full with joy.

But as the days passed, Lila began to notice strange changes. Her brother, who had left for the city, returned, but not as the same person. He was distant, his eyes clouded with sorrow. Lila’s mother, though returned, seemed to fade into the background, her presence less vivid. The tree, once a source of hope, now felt like a burden.

One evening, as Lila sat beneath the oak, she made another wish. This time, she wished for more than just love and peace. She wished for the ability to change the world, to make the world a better place. The tree’s embers flared, and a sudden gust of wind swept through the village, scattering leaves and sending a ripple through the air.

The consequences were immediate. The village flourished, with new buildings, fresh crops, and a sense of unity that had never been seen before. But with this prosperity came a price. The tree’s glow dimmed, and the once-vibrant embers now flickered with a sorrowful light. The villagers, though happy, began to notice that the tree’s energy was fading, and the whispers that once filled the air grew quieter.

Lila, now older and wiser, understood the truth: wishes, while beautiful, carried weight. They could bring joy, but they could also unravel the very fabric of life. The Whispering Oak, once a beacon of hope, became a reminder of the delicate balance between desire and responsibility.

And so, the villagers learned to cherish the tree, not for the wishes it granted, but for the lessons it taught. Lila, too, grew into a woman who understood the power of her choices, and the enduring truth that magic, like life, is both wondrous and fleeting.
